title: A stochastic model for elastoplastic contact of rough surfaces incorporating scale-dependent hardness
authors: Yang Xu, Hengxu Song, Jianqiao Hu
abstract: The stress concentrations caused by inherent roughness of natural and manufactured surfaces often induce plastic deformation at contact interfaces, a challenge compounded by competing influences of the size effect of plastic deformation and self-affine rough surface topography. To address this, we developed a novel methodology based on stochastic theory using compounded Chapman-Kolmogorov equations, for the first time, to solve elastoplastic contact problems involving scale-dependent hardness. Our approach formulates three integral equations describing the evolution of probability density functions of elastic contact pressure, relative plastic contact area, and relative non-contact area across geometrical scales. We thoroughly investigate the effects of scale-dependent hardness on contact pressure distribution, relative elastic and plastic contact areas, and the area-to-load relationship. By adjusting various mechanical and material properties, our model predicts a smooth transition from linear elasticity to elastic-plastic behavior and finally to full plasticity. A key advancement is the derivation of a new topographic yield parameter incorporating a wider range of material and geometrical properties, aiding identification of contact status. Numerical solutions enable highly precise determination of elastic and plastic limits via curve-fitting, and we also provide a new diagram for rapid identification of contact status. This study pioneers a stochastic process framework for applying the compounded Chapman-Kolmogorov equation to rough surface contact analysis, and the integral equations characterizing how interfacial properties evolve with scale could offer valuable insights for other multidisciplinary fields where multiscale roughness is critical, such as earthquakes, electrical contact, and contact electrification.
summary: This paper pioneers a stochastic model for elastoplastic contact on rough surfaces by incorporating the crucial effect of scale-dependent hardness. It uses compounded Chapman-Kolmogorov equations to track how probability distributions governing contact pressure and area evolve across different observation scales. The resulting framework provides advanced diagnostics to map out the transition between linear elastic, elastoplastic, and fully plastic regimes.
main_problem: The study addresses stress concentrations caused by inherent surface roughness leading to plastic deformation at contact interfaces, complicated by size effects and self-affine topography.
main_result: The authors developed a stochastic framework using compounded Chapman-Kolmogorov equations to model the evolution of probability density functions describing elastic, plastic, and non-contact areas across geometrical scales.
method: The methodology involves formulating and solving three coupled integral equations derived from the compounded Chapman-Kolmogorov equation, which tracks probability flow as the observation scale changes.
model_or_system: The system is elastoplastic contact between rough surfaces. The model incorporates a novel element: scale-dependent hardness, $H(\zeta)$, which modifies the standard contact mechanics framework by introducing size effects into the yield criterion.
key_observables: Probability density functions (PDFs) for elastic contact pressure, relative plastic contact area ($A^*_{pl}$), and relative non-contact area ($A^*_{nc}$).
important_parameters: Scale-dependent hardness $H(\zeta)$, magnification $\zeta$, topographic yield parameter ($\omega$), Hurst exponent ($H$), and hardness exponent ($n$).
main_assumptions: The model builds upon stochastic process theory, though it notes that previous assumptions like 'no re-entry' are inaccurate in practice. Numerical solutions rely on discretizing the scale (magnification) axis.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate probability flow for both constant and scale-dependent hardness, showing reopened paths due to size effects. Contour plots map contact status regimes based on misfit parameters ($M_{le}, M_{fp}$), comparing results against prior literature.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical problem (rough surface elastoplasticity) to developing the core mathematical tool (compounded Chapman-Kolmogorov equation). It then details the derivation of integral equations, presents numerical solutions for various hardness regimes, and concludes by defining diagnostic parameters to map out contact status zones.
why_interesting: While focused on solid mechanics, the use of stochastic process theory, probability density function evolution across scales (Chapman-Kolmogorov), and multi-scale modeling techniques are highly relevant to non-equilibrium statistical physics problems in AMO or condensed matter systems where scale separation is key.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: A cross-sectional view of the purely normal contact between a rigid flat and a rough surface and the schematic of the probability density function of the contact pressure.
Figure 2: A graphical illustration of probability flow from P(p′, ζ′) to P(p, ζ) where hardness is a constant. The three PDFs at the bottom are all replicas of P(p′, ζ′).
Figure 3: A graphical illustration of probability flow from P(p′, ζ′) to P(p, ζ) where hardness is scale-dependent. The three PDFs at bottom are all replicas of P(p′, ζ′)
Figure 4: Convergence test of linear elastic contact solutions solved by Chapman-Kolmogorov (C-K) equation (solid lines) and Persson’s theory (dashed lines): (a) relative contact area A∗, (b) P0(p/ p

Figure 5: Convergence test of elastoplastic contact solutions with constant hardness solved by Chapman-Kolmogorov (C-K) equation (solid lines) and Persson’s theory (dashed lines): (a) relative elastic contact area A∗ el, (b) relative plastic contact area A∗ pl, (c) P0(p/H0, ζ). The magnification axis is discretized by various nζ. The mean pressure ¯p = 1.5 GPa, H0 = 3 GPa, C0 = 1 × 10−8 mm4, ql = 2π mm−1, H = 0.7, E = 200 GPa, ν = 0.33, ∆p0 = H0/99.
